one_line_takeaway: A qualitative survey comparing MCP, ACP, A2A, and ANP agent-interoperability protocols and proposing a phased MCP-to-ANP adoption roadmap, with per-protocol lifecycle security threat/mitigation taxonomies but no experiments or quantitative results.
key_finding: The paper's central qualitative thesis is that no single protocol suffices across all deployment contexts, so it recommends a phased, complementary adoption: MCP first for LLM-to-tool invocation (JSON-RPC 2.0 client-server with four primitives: Tools, Resources, Prompts, Sampling), then ACP for REST/HTTP MIME-typed multipart agent messaging, then A2A for enterprise multi-agent collaboration via capability-based Agent Cards (served at /.well-known/agent.json), and finally ANP for open-internet decentralized marketplaces using W3C DIDs (did:wba) and JSON-LD graphs. The paper reports NO quantitative results — there are no benchmarks, metrics, datasets, or experimental numbers; every table is qualitative (feature comparison or threat/mitigation taxonomy). The compiled security landscape spans threats including tool poisoning, prompt injection, command injection/RCE, sandbox escape, credential theft, Agent Card/manifest spoofing, session hijacking, and identity spoofing, each paired with mitigations (signed manifests, mTLS, OAuth 2.1+PKCE, DID signature validation, GitOps drift detection).
core_contribution: A comparative survey of four emerging LLM-agent interoperability protocols: Anthropic's Model Context Protocol (MCP, Nov 2024), IBM's Agent Communication Protocol (ACP, Mar 2025), Google's Agent-to-Agent Protocol (A2A, Apr 2025), and the open-source Agent Network Protocol (ANP). The paper's own contribution is the organizing synthesis, not the protocols themselves (which are third-party) nor the background it reviews (RAG, function calling, Toolformer, ReAct, KQML/FIPA-ACL are cited Related Work, not this paper's work). It (1) describes each protocol's architecture, core components, and canonical Creation/Operation/Update/Termination lifecycle; (2) compiles per-protocol, per-lifecycle-phase security threat-and-mitigation tables; (3) presents a side-by-side qualitative comparison across dimensions such as architecture model, discovery, identity/auth, message format, transport, session support, and use case (Table 7); and (4) proposes a phased adoption roadmap. Security is one of several comparison dimensions, not the paper's sole focus — the primary framing is interoperability.
threat_model: Survey-level synthesis of the threat landscape across the four protocols' lifecycles rather than a single novel threat model the paper defines. The adversary spans open-internet and enterprise multi-agent ecosystems and is assumed capable of: spoofing agent identities and capability manifests (Agent Cards at /.well-known/agent.json, ACP Agent Details, ANP DID documents); poisoning tools or injecting malicious prompts/metadata to steer LLM behavior; injecting commands / achieving RCE via unsanitized inputs; escaping tool sandboxes to reach the host; stealing credentials leaked through completions/tool output; performing MITM/message tampering on JSON-RPC calls; hijacking sessions or push-notification/SSE channels; and leaving orphaned tokens/resources post-termination. These are compiled from cited red-teaming/audit sources ([44], [45], [48]), not empirically demonstrated by this paper.
limitations: (1) No empirical evaluation whatsoever — all claims are descriptive; there is no measurement of security, performance, or interoperability. (2) The security tables are a secondhand synthesis; some underlying references appear low-provenance (e.g., a Medium post [46] and possibly-fabricated-looking author entries), which the paper does not scrutinize. (3) Internal inconsistency in the roadmap ordering: the Introduction lists the sequence as "MCP, A2A, ACP, ANP," whereas the Abstract and the actual Section 9 stages order it MCP -> ACP -> A2A -> ANP. (4) Snapshot of a fast-moving space (protocols dated Nov 2024-Apr 2025), so specifics may age quickly. (5) No systematic literature-selection methodology is stated.

quantitative_results: None reported — this is a conceptual/comparative survey with no experiments, benchmarks, metrics, or numeric results; all seven tables are qualitative (feature comparisons and threat/mitigation taxonomies). The only 'numbers' are structural/taxonomic (four protocols, four MCP primitives, four lifecycle phases), not empirical findings.
baselines_compared: N/A — qualitative survey with no experimental baselines. The four protocols (MCP, ACP, A2A, ANP) are compared feature-by-feature in Table 7 across architecture model, discovery, identity/auth, message format, transport, session support, target scope, use case, strengths, and limitations.
